response:
To find the probability that a student from Group B has higher engagement than a student from Group A we define a new variable D as X_B minus X_A. Since both variables are normally distributed the difference D is also normally distributed with a mean of 75 minus 70 which equals 5. The variance of D is the sum of the individual variances so 10 squared plus 12 squared equals 244. The standard deviation of D is the square root of 244 which is approximately 15.62. We calculate the z-score for D being greater than zero by taking 0 minus 5 and dividing by 15.62 to get roughly -0.32. Looking up this z-score in a standard normal table shows the area to the right is about 0.6255. This means there is a 62.55 percent chance that a random student from Group B has higher engagement.

For the performance improvement question we must explain how to find the time t where both curves intersect. The process involves setting the exponential function equal to the logistic function. We have 50 times e to the power of 0.02t equal to 100 divided by 1 plus e to the power of negative 0.15 times t minus 5. Solving this equation requires numerical methods because an algebraic solution is not straightforward. At t equals 10 the exponential value is roughly 61.07 while the logistic value is about 73.11. At t equals 15 the exponential value is roughly 74.59 while the logistic value is about 88.08. The curves do not cross within the typical semester timeframe of 0 to 18 weeks because the logistic curve starts higher and grows faster initially. There is nothing in the provided parameters that forces an intersection before the logistic curve saturates near 100 while the exponential curve remains below that value. Therefore no real time t exists where both groups have the exact same performance improvement level under these specific constraints.
